range_notes: Migrates through southern Alberta; breeds where milkweeds occur.
description: Iconic migratory butterfly. Caterpillars feed only on milkweeds (Asclepias). Listed as Endangered under SARA in Canada.
image_attribution: (c) Christine Young, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Christine Young

range_notes: Widespread across boreal and aspen parkland Alberta.
description: Large yellow-and-black swallowtail. Caterpillars feed on aspen, birch, willow, and chokecherry.
image_attribution: (c) Bex Goreham, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Bex Goreham

range_notes: Southwestern Alberta foothills and river valleys.
description: Closely related to the Canadian Tiger Swallowtail. Caterpillars feed on willow, cottonwood, and chokecherry.
image_attribution: (c) Don Loarie, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Don Loarie

description: Yellow-and-black swallowtail of open prairie. Prairie subspecies caterpillars feed on Artemisia (wild sages).
image_attribution: (c) lugachev_vitaly, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by lugachev_vitaly

range_notes: Common throughout Alberta wherever willows occur.
description: Dark butterfly with yellow wing borders; one of the first to emerge in spring. Caterpillars feed on willow, poplar, birch, and elm.

range_notes: Common in moist meadows and disturbed ground.
description: Orange-banded tortoiseshell butterfly. Caterpillars feed exclusively on stinging nettle (Urtica).
image_attribution: (c) Matt Muir, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Matt Muir

description: Mottled brown-and-orange tortoiseshell. Caterpillars feed on birch, aspen, and willow.
image_attribution: (c) D. Gordon E. Robertson, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

range_notes: Periodic immigrant; major irruptions every several years.
description: Cosmopolitan migrant butterfly. Caterpillars are generalists on Asteraceae, especially thistles.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Joan C. Hinojosa

description: Dark butterfly with red wing-bands. Caterpillars feed on stinging nettle.
image_attribution: (c) Gilles San Martin, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Gilles San Martin

description: Black butterfly with broad white wing-bands. Caterpillars feed on aspen, willow, and birch.

range_notes: Meadows and openings across central and southern Alberta.
description: Orange fritillary butterfly. Caterpillars feed only on native violets (Viola).
Smaller orange fritillary of higher elevations. Caterpillars feed only on violets.
Brown butterfly with eye-spots. Caterpillars feed on native grasses.
image_attribution: (c) Judy Gallagher, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Judy Gallagher

description: Tiny pale-blue butterfly. Caterpillars feed on flower buds of dogwoods, viburnums, and cherries.
Small blue butterfly with silvery underside. Caterpillars feed on lupines, vetches, and milkvetches.
image_attribution: (c) Owen Strickland, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Owen Strickland

description: Small tailed-blue butterfly. Caterpillars feed on legume seedpods (milkvetch, vetch).
image_attribution: (c) Matt Benotsch, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Matt Benotsch

description: Orange-and-brown skipper. Caterpillars feed on native bunchgrasses.
Large silk moth with eye-spots on hind wings. Caterpillars feed on birch, aspen, willow, and cherry.
image_attribution: (c) Trevor Van Loon, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Trevor Van Loon

description: Largest North American silk moth. Caterpillars feed on willow, cherry, birch, and maple.
image_attribution: (c) Ryan Hodnett, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Ryan Hodnett

description: Hovers like a hummingbird at flowers. Caterpillars feed on honeysuckle and snowberry.
Black-and-yellow day-flying moth. Caterpillars feed on snowberry and honeysuckle.
image_attribution: (c) Marvin Smith, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

range_notes: Widespread breeder; nomadic in winter following fruit.
description: Sleek crested songbird that feeds primarily on fruit. Strongly associated with serviceberry, mountain ash, and viburnum.
image_attribution: (c) Christian Back, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Christian Back

description: Bright yellow seed-eating finch. Heavily dependent on Asteraceae seed (sunflower, thistle, goldenrod, aster).
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Glenn Berry

range_notes: Year-round in boreal regions; irruptive elsewhere.
description: Streaked finch that feeds on birch, alder, and conifer seeds.
image_attribution: (c) Cephas, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

range_notes: Winter visitor; breeds in the far north.
description: Small streaky finch with red cap. Winter diet is dominated by birch and alder seeds.
image_attribution: (c) caroline legg, some rights reserved (CC BY)

range_notes: Summer breeder in central and northern Alberta.
description: Tiny migratory hummingbird. Feeds at red tubular flowers; bee balm and columbine are major nectar sources.
Aggressive coppery hummingbird of the mountain west. Feeds at red tubular flowers.
Insectivorous songbird; nestlings depend almost entirely on caterpillars. Native trees rich in lepidoptera (aspen, willow, birch, cherry) anchor its food supply.
image_attribution: (c) Blake Ross, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Blake Ross

range_notes: Summer breeder; strongly tied to riparian willow and dogwood thickets.
description: Bright yellow warbler that nests almost exclusively in willow and dogwood shrubs along streams and wetland edges.
image_attribution: (c) Aitor, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Aitor

description: Familiar thrush. Eats earthworms in spring/summer; switches to fruit (serviceberry, mountain ash, chokecherry, hawthorn) in fall and winter.
image_attribution: (c) Judy Gallagher, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Large social bees critical for cold-climate pollination. Generalist foragers; particularly important on lupines, bergamot, asters, and goldenrods.

range_notes: Diverse genus with many Alberta species.
description: Solitary ground-nesting bees; many are spring specialists tied to willow catkins, early native shrubs, and asters.
Solitary bees that nest in cavities (bee hotels). Important pollinators of fruit trees and shrubs.
Solitary bees that cut leaf disks to line nests. Strong foragers on legumes and composites.
Small to medium ground-nesting bees, often metallic green. Highly generalist on composites and other prairie flowers.
Common prairie/parkland bumble bee; long colony season makes it a key generalist pollinator of asters, clovers and mints.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by USGS Bee Inventory and Monitoring Lab

description: Orange-banded bumble bee of boreal and parkland edges; forages willow, blueberry, goldenrod.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Kent McFarland

description: Highly variable short-tongued bumble bee common in gardens and grassland.
Long-tongued bumble bee favouring deep flowers (legumes, mints).
Spring solitary bee; superb fruit-tree and willow pollinator that nests in cavities (and bee hotels).
image_attribution: (c) Harley Hosford, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Harley Hosford

description: Solitary cavity-nester that cuts leaf discs (often from rose) to line its nest cells; strong summer pollinator of legumes and asters.
image_attribution: (c) Owen Strickland, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Owen Strickland

description: Metallic-green communal ground-nesting bee; generalist on open flowers.
image_attribution: (c) Christina Butler, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Hugely diverse genus of tiny ground-nesting generalist pollinators — among the most abundant bees in any Alberta garden.
Early ground-nesting bee that lines burrows with a cellophane-like secretion; vital willow and early-spring pollinator.
Social sweat bee nesting in bare soil; generalist on composites.
image_attribution: (c) Michelle Orcutt, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Michelle Orcutt

description: Fast-flying solitary digger bee; long tongue suits penstemons and mints.
image_attribution: (c) Amy Schnebelin, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Amy Schnebelin

description: Open-country raptor that nests in tall poplars and spruce and hunts small mammals over meadows.
Alberta's provincial bird; nests in poplar/spruce, controls rodents.
image_attribution: (c) Ben Hayden, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Ben Hayden

description: Small falcon that reuses old crow/magpie nests in spruce; hunts small birds in towns and parkland.
Cavity-nesting little falcon of open country; eats grasshoppers, voles.
image_attribution: (c) Jonathan Eisen, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Jonathan Eisen

description: Smallest woodpecker; forages insects on shrubs and trees and excavates nest cavities in soft dead wood (poplar, birch).
image_attribution: (c) Alexis Williams, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Alexis Williams

description: Ground-foraging woodpecker that eats ants and autumn fruit; nests in aspen/poplar snags.
image_attribution: (c) Jon Robson, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Jon Robson

description: Bold parkland corvid; caches acorns and conifer seeds and takes fruit.
image_attribution: (c) Judy Gallagher, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Ubiquitous corvid; omnivore whose bulky thorny nests shelter other species after use.
image_attribution: (c) Rose Zappa, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Rose Zappa

description: Boreal/foothills jay that caches food in spruce; year-round resident.
image_attribution: (c) Owen Strickland, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Owen Strickland

description: Conifer specialist that eats spruce/pine seeds and bark insects; nests in cavities.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Claude Lyneis

description: Shrub-and-edge sparrow; eats grass and forb seeds and nests low in dense cover.
image_attribution: (c) Daniel Levitis, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Daniel Levitis

description: Ground-feeding sparrow of forest edges; winters on fallen weed seeds.
image_attribution: (c) Becky Matsubara, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Large winter finch that feeds on mountain-ash and other persistent fruit plus conifer and birch seeds.
Winter-flocking frugivore that strips berries from saskatoon, highbush-cranberry, hawthorn and rose.
image_attribution: (c) Fyn Kynd, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Fyn Kynd

description: Aspen-woodland grouse that eats poplar/willow buds, catkins and shrub fruit; broods need insect-rich understory.
image_attribution: (c) Alexis Godin, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Alexis Godin

description: Honey-bee-mimicking hover fly (introduced from Europe, now widespread); adults are major generalist pollinators of composites and umbels.
image_attribution: (c) Drepanostoma, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Drepanostoma

description: Hover fly whose aphid-eating larvae are valuable biocontrol; adults take pollen and nectar.
image_attribution: (c) Virginia Heatwole, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Virginia Heatwole

description: Tiny hover fly; aphid-predator larvae, pollinator adults.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Jesse Rorabaugh

description: Native lady beetle; both adults and larvae eat aphids, adults also take pollen and nectar.
Declining native lady beetle; aphid predator of grassland and gardens.
'Aphid lions' as larvae; adults sip nectar and honeydew. Key garden biocontrol.
Night-active predators of slugs, cutworms and weed seeds; shelter in leaf litter and groundcover.
Pollen- and nectar-feeding beetle of late-summer goldenrod; larvae are soil predators.
image_attribution:
(c) 
Wildreturn, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Large migratory dragonfly; aerial predator of mosquitoes and flies, needs ponds with emergent sedges.
image_attribution: (c) Eugene Zelenko, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

description: Pond dragonfly of marsh edges; voracious insect predator.
image_attribution: (c) Peter Preus, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Peter Preus

description: Late-season red dragonfly of wet meadows and pond margins.
image_attribution: (c) Nick Block, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Nick Block

description: Slender damselfly of vegetated still water; larvae and adults prey on small insects.
image_attribution: (c) Diego González Dopico, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Diego González Dopico

description: Insectivorous bat eating thousands of mosquitoes nightly; roosts under bark and in tree cavities. SARA-listed (Endangered).
image_attribution: (c) SMBishop, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

description: Hardy urban-tolerant bat; forages beetles and moths over meadows and water.
image_attribution: (c) Juan Cruzado Cortés, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Juan Cruzado Cortés

description: Migratory tree bat roosting under bark of poplar and spruce.
Abundant native seed-eater and key prey for owls, hawks and foxes; disperses seeds and fungi.
image_attribution: (c) Don Loarie, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Don Loarie

description: Grassland herbivore whose runways shape meadows; cornerstone prey for raptors and weasels.
Tiny voracious insectivore of moist litter; eats insects, slugs and larvae.
image_attribution: (c) Dan MacNeal, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Dan MacNeal

description: Day-and-dusk hawkmoth; a hummingbird-like nectar pollinator of tubular flowers; larvae feed on flax and evening-primrose family plants.
image_attribution: (c) Michelle W. （鍾偉瑋）, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Michelle W. （鍾偉瑋）

description: Large hawkmoth whose caterpillars feed on poplar and willow foliage.
image_attribution: (c) Ian Manning, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Ian Manning

description: Eyed hawkmoth; larvae host on willow, birch and cherry.
image_attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by kent ozment

description: The 'woolly bear' caterpillar; a generalist on asters, goldenrods and many forbs that overwinters as a larva.
Tussock caterpillar feeding on willow, poplar, alder and birch.
image_attribution: (c) Paul Bedell, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Paul Bedell

description: Large skipper; caterpillars host on native legumes (milkvetch, vetch).
image_attribution: (c) christine123, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by christine123

description: Abundant grassland butterfly; caterpillars feed on native grasses.
Ubiquitous yellow butterfly; caterpillars host on legumes (vetch, milkvetch, lupine).
image_attribution: (c) Jimmy Dee, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Jimmy Dee

description: Prairie blue whose caterpillars feed on legumes and are tended by ants.
Small orange-and-black butterfly; caterpillars host on native asters.
Orange-banded Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Nests on the surface or underground. Forages on Agastache, Cirsium, Delphinium, Gentiana and others.
Mountain Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Castilleja, Delphinium, Epilobium, Mertensia and others.
Two-ranked Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests usually underground, sometimes on the surface. Forages on Aster, Epilobium, Haplopappus, Lupinus and others.

range_notes: Occurs in Alberta. At risk (COSEWIC: Endangered).
description: Gypsy Cuckoo Bumble Bee. A cuckoo (Psithyrus) bumble bee — produces no workers and usurps the colonies of other Bombus. Adults nectar at Cirsium, Melilotus, Rubus.
Central Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Allium, Cirsium, Monardella, Penstemon and others.
Cryptic Bumble Bee. A short-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Epilobium, Melilotus, Potentilla, Senecio and others.

Golden Northern Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Nests usually on the surface or above ground in deserted mouse nests, tall grass or haystacks. Forages on Astragalus, Cirsium, Helianthus, Lonicera and others.
Yellow Cuckoo Bumble Bee. A cuckoo (Psithyrus) bumble bee — produces no workers and usurps the colonies of other Bombus. Adults nectar at Cirsium, Melilotus, Senecio.
Yellow-fronted Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Cirsium, Epilobium, Mentha, Penstemon and others.
Frigid Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests often above ground, sometimes below. Forages on Epilobium, Hedysarum, Lupinus, Potentilla and others.
Brown-belted Bumble Bee. A short-tongued social bumble bee. Nests underground or on the surface. Forages on Asclepias, Echinacea, Lythrum, Melilotus and others.
Indiscriminate Cuckoo Bumble Bee. A cuckoo (Psithyrus) bumble bee — produces no workers and usurps the colonies of other Bombus. Adults nectar at Aster, Melilotus, Rubus.

Black-tailed Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests underground and above ground, sometimes in birdhouses or building insulation. Forages on Arctostaphylos, Eriogonum, Haplopappus, Lupinus and others.

Brown-tailed Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests variable — many on the ground surface, but also above and below ground. Forages on Epilobium, Monardella, Penstemon, Phacelia and others.
Nevada Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Astragalus, Cirsium, Melilotus, Monarda and others.

range_notes: Occurs in Alberta. At risk (COSEWIC: Threatened).
description: Western Bumble Bee. A short-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Centaurea, Cirsium, Geranium, Grindelia and others.

range_notes: Occurs in Alberta. At risk (COSEWIC: Special Concern).
description: American Bumble Bee. A long-tongued social bumble bee. Nests usually on the surface in long grass, sometimes underground. Forages on Astragalus, Cirsium, Cornus, Echinacea and others.
Confusing Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Campanula, Cirsium, Melilotus, Penstemon and others.
Northern Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Arnica, Pedicularis, Phyllodoce, Polemonium and others.
Sitka Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Forages on Cirsium, Ribes, Vaccinium and others.

range_notes: Occurs in Alberta. At risk (COSEWIC: Threatened).
description: Suckley's Cuckoo Bumble Bee. A cuckoo (Psithyrus) bumble bee — produces no workers and usurps the colonies of other Bombus. Adults nectar at Aster, Cirsium, Solidago.
Red-tailed Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests mostly underground, sometimes on the surface. Forages on Epilobium, Haplopappus, Lupinus, Monardella and others.

range_notes: Occurs in Alberta. At risk (COSEWIC: Special Concern).
description: Yellow-banded Bumble Bee. A short-tongued social bumble bee. Nests usually underground. Forages on Crocus, Linaria, Melilotus, Monarda and others.
Half-black Bumble Bee. A medium-tongued social bumble bee. Nests usually underground. Forages on Aster, Cirsium, Lonicera, Monarda and others.
Bumblebee-like Digger Bee. A fast-flying, soil-nesting solitary digger bee. Foraging: a broad generalist (polylectic).
Occidental Digger Bee. A fast-flying, soil-nesting solitary digger bee. Foraging: a broad generalist (polylectic).
Rocky Mountain Beeplant Digger Bee. A fast-flying, soil-nesting solitary digger bee. Foraging: a pollen specialist on Grindelia.
Porter's Digger Bee. A fast-flying, soil-nesting solitary digger bee. Foraging: a broad generalist (polylectic).
Bear-like Digger Bee. A fast-flying, soil-nesting solitary digger bee. Foraging: a broad generalist (polylectic).
Southern Chimney Bee. Solitary, soil-nesting bee; a pollen specialist on Opuntia. Females build turret-like 'chimney' nest entrances.
Diminutive Chimney Bee. Solitary, soil-nesting bee; a pollen specialist on Sphaeralcea. Females build turret-like 'chimney' nest entrances.
Ochre-footed Long-horned Bee. Long-horned solitary bee; soil nester and broad generalist forager.
Eagle Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Articulated Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Beautiful Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Lehigh Gap Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Snow's Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
True Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Bounded Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Andrena bees and builds no nest of its own.
Mountain Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Concave Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Dakota Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Sunflower Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Yellow-breasted Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Subalpine Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Melissodes bees and builds no nest of its own.
American Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Colletes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Compact Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Colletes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Minimal Cuckoo Nomad Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Colletes bees and builds no nest of its own.
Pacific Cuckoo Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Anthophora bees and builds no nest of its own.
Speckled Cuckoo Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Anthophora bees and builds no nest of its own.
California Cuckoo Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Anthophora bees and builds no nest of its own.
Maculate Cuckoo Bee. A cleptoparasitic 'cuckoo' bee — it lays in the nests of Anthophora bees and builds no nest of its own.
Long-horned bees — late-summer ground nesters strongly tied to Asteraceae (sunflowers, asters, coneflowers).
